title: How Many Names Does This Government Have?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked derived reading copy in which clear OCR character, punctuation, and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Under AIADMK rule, the flag of “corruption” flies higher and higher! One does not know which corruption to describe and which to leave aside. Open a newspaper and page after page carries the Tamil Nadu government's corruption. The Public Works Department stands first even in corruption. Is it not under the Chief Minister? Naturally it has captured first place.

Ezhilagam on Chennai's beach road houses many government offices. At its entrance stood a huge digital banner. It had not been placed anonymously overnight. It expressly named the Tamil Nadu Public Works Engineering Contractors' Association, Chennai. Do you know its heading? “Who is the No. 1 engineer responsible for massive corruption in Tamil Nadu's Public Works Department in 2014-15?”

The PWD accounts for enormous expenditure in the State Budget. Through it, crores are spent on government buildings, hospitals, dams, canals, lakes and tanks. Fifteen thousand contractors execute such work across Tamil Nadu, including six hundred in Chennai. Complaints have long alleged that people connected with the ruling party, from the highest to the lowest level, demand shares amounting to 35 to 45 per cent commission. What greater shame does this government need than the contractors' own association placing a banner at Ezhilagam asking who the foremost corrupt engineer is? What does it mean when the association boldly says it will announce the name on a billboard? The Minister responsible for this department is Tamil Nadu's Chief Minister!
There is more. A telephone conversation about financial dealings between a PWD contractor and an official has circulated through WhatsApp and newspapers including Dinamalar and Dinakaran.

Association State President Gunamani and Treasurer Kumar publicly said they would release a list of the ten PWD officials taking the most bribes. Their interview stated: “To undertake contract work, a 35 per cent commission must be distributed among the Chief Engineer, Superintending Engineer, Executive Engineer, Assistant Executive Engineer, Assistant Engineer and Works Inspector. A further five per cent must be paid to an important figure in the department. For two years, besides payment to that figure, another five per cent has been demanded for the important figure connected with medical works. In total we pay 45 per cent commission, two per cent income tax and two per cent sales tax. When we said proper work could not be done with what remained, the important medical-department figure began giving contracts to a friend from Pudukkottai. We receive neither work nor payment for work completed over the past thirteen months.”
Is this a government or daylight robbery conducted in the government's name? Are public offices commercial centres or auction houses?

Who is the important figure receiving five per cent? Who is the Pudukkottai friend receiving contracts? Is the 45 per cent allegation true? If 45 per cent of a project's value is paid as bribes, what quality can the remaining money purchase? How badly must a contractor execute the work? When a block of ice passes through hand after hand, will even a drop remain at the end? What is the AIADMK government's answer?

The association also said: “Officials and their families tour Britain, France and Turkey on bribe money. In 2014-15, Rs 60 crore was allotted to maintain hospitals and other buildings. Officials say all of it was spent, but proper work occurred nowhere. Irregularities also occurred in funds for the medical college hospital built at Omandurar Government Estate. We have had enough. We will complain today to the anti-corruption and vigilance authorities and, if they do not act, release the ten officials' names.” Their public statement shows the depth of their frustration.

Who were the officials who travelled abroad on bribe money? If the contractors' association says allotted maintenance funds were shown as spent although work was neither proper nor complete, what better evidence is needed of how taxpayers' money is wasted under AIADMK rule? When I was in office and the Secretariat and Assembly complex was being built at Omandurar, I visited daily, inspected the work and advised officials. The AIADMK government made sweeping allegations and filed a case against us. Yet office-bearers of the contractors' association now allege irregularities in funds for the medical college and hospital built there under the AIADMK. What is its answer?
The government attempted to pacify the contractors. A senior Buildings Division official called them in, spoke for an hour and urged them not to enlarge the issue, seeking to quiet and conceal it.

As though bearing witness to the corruption, newspapers published an entire telephone conversation between a PWD official and a contractor. The official asks why promised money was not paid, why it was delayed, says he advanced Rs 90,000 of his own money and is tormented, and says that if he does not recover it he does not know what he will do and his life may end like Muthukumarasamy's. He asks whether Karthik, Lenin and others did not pay properly. He addresses the contractor as Krishnamurthy. Who is Krishnamurthy? Who is the official? Why is money demanded, for whom and for what work? The AIADMK government must immediately answer Tamil Nadu's people.

Dinamalar is not a DMK newspaper. Today, 8 May 2015, under the heading “A New Name for the Tamil Nadu Government,” it wrote: “The Tamil Nadu government, already called a proxy government, inactive government, puppet government and advisers' government, has now acquired another name: the ‘45 per cent government.’ Contractors' allegation that 45 per cent of every PWD contract must be paid as commission has shaken Tamil Nadu. The question arises: after paying 45 per cent commission, what will be the quality of the work?” It also published reactions from prominent people.

PWD contractors' association office-bearers told Dinakaran yesterday: “Even before contract work begins, commission must be paid to officials and up to the Minister. Otherwise we are not allowed to tender. Recently, interference by an important person's son has increased. Contracts go only to those he indicates. On the fourth, tenders worth Rs 90 crore were issued for Krishna Canal repairs, two works in the Kosasthalaiyar and Araniyar divisions, and Cooum restoration. On the important person's son's recommendation, contracts went to Madurai Manickam, Jayakumar, Subbaiah of Theni, a Tiruvallur firm and an Erode firm. Not one is a Chennai-region contractor.” Who is this “important person's son”? Were works awarded to those he indicated or not? Must the government not answer?

Such corruption lies thick in every department under AIADMK rule. Only now has it begun to emerge with explicit evidence. What are the Ministers doing? Only one year remains, and they seem to work on the principle that whatever can be extracted before then is profit. They visit temple after temple, conduct rituals, pull chariots and walk over fire. They say it is for Jayalalithaa's acquittal, but it is reportedly really to save their own posts and avoid being caught. How many Collectors participate in these ceremonies and renovations? Are they IAS officers or ruling-party attendants? Why have neither the Chief Secretary nor the Governor acted? They may escape today, but a day will certainly come when they must answer before a commission of inquiry.

As soon as news of the Ezhilagam banner arrived, police tried to remove it. When the association objected to that banner alone being removed, police said they would remove every banner in the area. But neither the police nor the police “adviser” who leads them in propping up the AIADMK government can hide this regime's corruption. Some people may be deceived for many days, and many people for a few days, but everyone cannot be deceived every day. People have begun to understand the truth. They can no longer be diverted by asking, “Will you do it? Will you do it?”
